title: Business banking on boost from AI
full_text:
Adopting artificial intelligence remains the main way to reverse the biggest productivity slump in a generation despite the technology generating “workplace slop” and its cost blowing out corporate budgets within months, Australian business leaders have said.
Executives from BHP, Uber and TechnologyOne told Australia’s Economic Outlook forum in Sydney on Friday that AI was a groundbreaking technology that could dramatically lift employee output.
BHP Australia president Geraldine Slattery said the miner was using AI to spot a foreign object or oversized rock on a conveyor before it forces a shutdown. A stoppage of six to 12 hours costs the iron ore giant 450,000 tonnes at about $US100 ($140.30) a tonne.
“The state loses out on royalties, shareholders lose out on profits, you can detect that and intervene, you can prevent those kind of multi-hour stoppages,” Ms Slattery told the forum, hosted by Sky News and The Australian. “So (there are) enormous gains from actually quite incremental investments.” Australia has recorded one of the worst productivity performances among developed economies since Covid, and has underperformed the long-term average over the past decade.
Asked whether those investments were replacing workers as BHP faces its first industrial dispute in the Pilbara since the 1960s, Ms Slattery said people needed to look at the big picture.
“It (AI) opens up greater productivity, but it also opens up new roles, and we’ve seen that in the journey to autonomy on our mine,” she said. “Whilst you may not have as many truck operators as you once had, you have autonomous officers, you have remote operations centres, so the workforce has evolved and will continue to evolve as we take on technologies.” Uber burned through its AI budget with Claude maker Anthropic – the frontier model company that is valued at almost $US1 trillion and preparing a blockbuster public offering – in just four months.
It has adopted Anthropic’s coding tool, Claude Code, which has disrupted the software engineering sector and has been a factor behind the so-called SaaSpocalypse, sparking steep share price falls and job losses at companies including Atlassian, Afterpay owner Block and WiseTech Global.
Uber retail regional general manager Lucas Groeneveld said the company had since topped up its Anthropic budget.
“If we fall asleep at the wheel on this as a nation we’re in big trouble … both in terms of our productivity, but also competing on the global scale,” he said.
“I’ve definitely produced some of my own AI slop, so I’m guilty, guilty as charged. But I think what we found overnight is workers have grown a third arm, right, and almost overnight you have this access to produce, analyse, prepare data, and people are figuring out how to use it. The models themselves will get better, people will get better at prompting, and people will learn how to use this third arm. Ultimately that drives productivity.” Australia is in the midst of its worst labour productivity levels on record with average productivity growth over the past decade hitting its lowest levels in 60 years. The Productivity Commission has warned Australia is now stuck in a malaise, with the workforce barely more productive now than it was prior to the Covid-19 pandemic.
While WiseTech founder and executive chairman Richard White said an AI agent could do a job as well as a human, TechnologyOne executive vice-president of sales Barry Dietrich said it wasn’t so clear cut.
“It’s such a trust issue. Do I trust the result that gets surfaced from the model?” he said.
Mr Dietrich cast the technology as an addition rather than a replacement. “We can develop more and keep up with the demands of our customers. They expect that they want to see the new technology continually evolving and coming through, so for us it’s been a real plus one,” he said. “It’s been that third arm, it’s helped us accelerate.”
